content:
A community generates 1 million litres/day (MLD) of wastewater. This wastewater is treated using activated sludge process (ASP). The working volume of the aeration tank of the ASP is $250 \mathrm{~m}^3$, and the biomass concentration in the tank is $3000 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{L}$. Analyses results showed that a biomass concentration of $10 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{L}$ is present in the treated effluent from the secondary sedimentation tank of the ASP. Sludge wastage from the system is at a rate of $5000 \mathrm{~L} /$ day with a biomass concentration of $10000 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{L}$. The system is in steady state condition.

The biological sludge residence time (BSRT) of the system (in days) is _________ (round off to one decimal place).
explanation:
To calculate the Biological Sludge Residence Time (BSRT) or sludge age ($\theta_c$) in days, the formula used is:

$ \theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_u + (Q_0 - Q_w) \cdot X_e} $

Where:

$ Q_0 = 1 \times 10^6 \, \text{L/day} $ (total wastewater flow rate)

$ V = 250 \, \text{m}^3 $ (volume of the aeration tank)

$ X = 3000 \, \text{mg/L} $ (biomass concentration)

$ X_e = 10 \, \text{mg/L} $ (biomass concentration in treated effluent)

$ Q_w = 5000 \, \text{L/day} $ (sludge wastage flow rate)

$ X_u = 10000 \, \text{mg/L} $ (biomass concentration in waste sludge)

Substituting the values:

$ \theta_c = \frac{250 \times 3000 \times 10^3}{5000 \times 10000 + (10^6 - 5000) \times 10} $

Calculating step-by-step:

Calculate the numerator: $ 250 \times 3000 \times 10^3 = 750 \times 10^6 $.

Calculate the denominator: $ 5000 \times 10000 + (10^6 - 5000) \times 10 $.

For the denominator:

$ 5000 \times 10000 = 50 \times 10^6 $

$ (10^6 - 5000) \times 10 = 995 \times 10^3 $

Adding these: $ 50 \times 10^6 + 9.95 \times 10^6 = 59.95 \times 10^6 $.

Thus, the sludge age ($\theta_c$) is:

$ \theta_c = \frac{750 \times 10^6}{59.95 \times 10^6} \approx 12.5 \, \text{days} $

Therefore, the Biological Sludge Residence Time is approximately 12.5 days.

content: A facultative pond system is employed for wastewater treatment. Which of the following statements is/are true:

options.content: The dissolved oxygen concentration will be high during daytime compared to nighttime
The pH will be high during daytime compared to night-time
The dissolved oxygen concentration will be low during daytime compared to nighttime
The pH will be low during daytime compared to night-time

explanation: During day time photosynthesis takes place, algae produces oxygen and consumes $\mathrm{CO}_2$, therefore pH will increase and dissolved oxygen will also increase. Hence, the dissolved oxygen concentration will be high during daytime compared to night-time and the pH will be high during daytime compared to night-time.

content:
In the following table, identify the correct set of associations between the entries in Column - 1 and Column-2.

Column-1 Column-2
P: Reverse Osmosis III: Concentration Polarization
Q: Trickling Filter I: Ponding
R: Coagulation IV: Charge Neutralization
S: Adsorption II: Freundlich Isotherm

options.content: P - II, Q - I, S - III
Q - III, R - II, S - IV
P - IV, R - I, S - II
P - III, Q - I, R - IV

explanation:
The correct set of associations between Column-1 and Column-2 is Option D: P - III, Q - I, R - IV.

Let's break down why:

Column-1 Column-2 Explanation
P: Reverse Osmosis III: Concentration Polarization

Reverse Osmosis (RO) is a membrane filtration process where a semi-permeable membrane separates water from contaminants. Concentration Polarization is a phenomenon that occurs in RO where the rejected contaminants accumulate near the membrane surface, reducing the efficiency of the process.
Q: Trickling Filter I: Ponding

Trickling filters are a wastewater treatment method where wastewater is distributed over a bed of media (rocks, plastic, etc.). Microorganisms on the media break down organic matter. Ponding occurs when the spaces between the media become clogged, leading to poor treatment efficiency.
R: Coagulation IV: Charge Neutralization

Coagulation is a water treatment process used to remove suspended particles. Coagulants with a high charge density are added to the water, which neutralizes the negative charges of the suspended particles, allowing them to clump together (flocculation) and settle out or be removed by filtration.
S: Adsorption II: Freundlich Isotherm

Adsorption is a process where contaminants adhere to the surface of a material, such as activated carbon. The Freundlich Isotherm is an equation that describes the relationship between the amount of adsorbate (contaminant) adsorbed onto the adsorbent and the equilibrium concentration of the adsorbate in the solution.

content: The wastewater inflow to an activated sludge plant is 0.5 m3/s, and the plant is to be operated with a food to microorganism ratio of 0.2 mg/mg-d. The concentration of influent biodegradable organic matter of the wastewater to the plant (after primary settling) is 150 mg/L, and the mixed liquor volatile suspended solids concentration to be maintained in the plant is 2000 mg/L. Assuming that complete removal of biodegradable organic matter in the tank, the volume of aeration tank (in m3, in integer) required for the plant is _________.
explanation:
To determine the volume of the aeration tank required for the given activated sludge plant, we can use the following relationship involving the Food to Microorganism ratio (F/M ratio):

$$ \text{F/M ratio} = \frac{(Q \cdot S_0)}{(V \cdot X)} $$

Where:

  • $Q$ = Flow rate of wastewater (m3/day)
  • $S_0$ = Concentration of influent biodegradable organic matter (mg/L)
  • $V$ = Volume of the aeration tank (m3)
  • $X$ = Mixed liquor volatile suspended solids concentration (mg/L)
  • $F/M$ = Food to Microorganism ratio (mg/mg-day)

First, convert the flow rate from m3/s to m3/day:

$$ Q = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 86400 \, \text{s/day} = 43200 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$

Next, substitute the given values into the F/M ratio formula:

$$ 0.2 = \frac{(43200 \cdot 150)}{(V \cdot 2000)} $$

Solve for $V$:

$$ 0.2 = \frac{6480000}{V \cdot 2000} $$

$$ 0.2 \cdot V \cdot 2000 = 6480000 $$

$$ V = \frac{6480000}{0.2 \cdot 2000} $$

$$ V = 16200 \, \text{m}^3 $$

Thus, the volume of the aeration tank required for the plant, in integer form, is:

16200 m3

content:
In a city, the chemical formula of biodegradable fraction of municipal solid waste (MSW) is C100H250O80N. The waste has to be treated by forced-aeration composting process for which air requirement has to be estimated.

Assume oxygen in air (by weight) = 23% and density of air = 1.3 kg/m3. Atomic mass : C = 12, H = 1, O = 16, N = 14.

C and H are oxidized completely whereas N is converted only into NH3 during oxidation.

For oxidative degradation of 1 tonne of the waste, the required theoretical volume of air (in m3/tonne) will be (round off the nearest integer)
explanation:
$$\mathop {{C_{100}}{H_{250}}{O_{80}}N}\limits_{2744g} + \mathop {121.75{O_2}}\limits_{3896g} \to 100C{O_2} + 123.5{H_2}O + N{H_3}$$

$$\therefore$$ if 1 tonne (or 1000 kg) of waste is present,

oxygen required = $$\left( {{{3896} \over {2744}}} \right) \times 100$$ kg = 1419.825 kg

Now, 1.3 kg of air contains = 0.23 $$\times$$ 1.3 kg = 0.299 kg of oxygen

$$\therefore$$ 1419.825 kg of oxygen will be containd by = $$\left( {{{1.3} \over {0.299}}} \right) \times 1419.825$$

= 6173.152 kg of air

Volume of air $$ = {{6173.152} \over {1.3}} = 4748.57$$ m3

content: A sewage treatment plant receives sewage at a flow rate of 5000 m3/day. The total suspended solids (TSS) concentration in the sewage at the inlet of primary clarifier is 200 mg/L. After the primary treatment, the TSS concentration in sewage is reduced by 60%. The sludge from the primary clarifier contains 2% solids concentration. Subsequently, the sludge is subjected to gravity thickening process to achieve a solids concentration of 6%. Assume that the density of sludge, before and after thickening, is 1000 kg/m3. The daily volume of the thickened sludge (in m3/day) will be _________. (round off to the nearest integer)
explanation:
Given : Sewage flow rate $=5000 \mathrm{~m}^3 /$ day

Total suspended solid concentration $=200 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{l}$

Primary treatment TSS concentration reduced by $60 \%$

Before primary clarifier contains $2 \%$ solid concentration

After thickening $6 \%$ solid concentration

Density $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$

$$ =\frac{5000 \times 10^3 \times 200}{10^6} \times \frac{60}{100}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \text { day } $$

100 kg sludge $\rightarrow 6 \%$ solid

$\rightarrow 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ solid forms 100 kg sludge

$\rightarrow 1 \mathrm{~kg}$ solid forms $\frac{100}{6} \mathrm{~kg}$ sludge

$\rightarrow 600 \mathrm{~kg}$ solid forms $\frac{100}{6} \times 600 \Rightarrow 10^4 \mathrm{~kg}$ of sludge

Volume $=\frac{w}{\rho}=\frac{10^4}{10^3}=10 \mathrm{~m}^3 /$ day

content:
A wastewater sample contains two nitrogen species, namely ammonia and nitrate. Consider the atomic weight of N, H, and O as 14 g/mol, 1 g/mol, and 16 g/mol, respectively. In this wastewater, the concentration of ammonia is 34 mg NH3/liter and that of nitrate is 6.2 mg NO$$_3^ - $$/liter. The total nitrogen concentration in this wastewater is _________ milligram nitrogen per liter.

(round off to one decimal place)
explanation:
To determine the total nitrogen concentration in the wastewater, the nitrogen content in each species must be calculated and then summed.

Ammonia (NH$_3$):

The molecular weight of NH$_3$ is calculated as follows:

$$ 14 \, \text{(N)} + 3 \times 1 \, \text{(H)} = 17 \, \text{g/mol} $$

The concentration of ammonia is 34 mg NH$_3$/liter. Thus, the nitrogen content from ammonia is:

$$ \left( \frac{14}{17} \right) \times 34 \, \text{mg N/liter} = 28 \, \text{mg N/liter} $$

Nitrate (NO$_3^-$):

The molecular weight of NO$_3^-$ is calculated as follows:

$$ 14 \, \text{(N)} + 3 \times 16 \, \text{(O)} = 62 \, \text{g/mol} $$

The concentration of nitrate is 6.2 mg NO$_3^-$/liter. Thus, the nitrogen content from nitrate is:

$$ \left( \frac{14}{62} \right) \times 6.2 \, \text{mg N/liter} \approx 1.4 \, \text{mg N/liter} $$

Total Nitrogen Concentration:

The total nitrogen concentration in the wastewater is the sum of the nitrogen content from ammonia and nitrate:

$$ 28 \, \text{mg N/liter} + 1.4 \, \text{mg N/liter} = 29.4 \, \text{mg N/liter} $$

Therefore, the total nitrogen concentration in the wastewater is 29.4 milligram nitrogen per liter.

29.4 mg N/liter
